source_inspiration: IMO seed — pigeonhole principle for young learners
stem_template: A bag has {R} red sweets and {B} blue sweets. {name} reaches in WITHOUT looking. What is the smallest number of sweets {name} must take to be SURE of having 2 the same colour?

visual_manifest.art_brief: A cloth bag with a question mark on it. Beside it, {R} red sweets and {B} blue sweets are shown outside the bag for reference. {name} reaches in with one hand.
visual_manifest.alt_text: A bag containing red and blue sweets.

misconceptions.feedback_child: 2 MIGHT work — but what if you get 1 red and 1 blue? Not sure yet!
Worst case: 1 red, 1 blue = 2 colours. The 3rd MUST match one!
You don't need them all! With 2 colours, the 3rd sweet must match.

socratic_feedback.on_correct: Brilliant! With 2 colours, the 3rd pick MUST match one. That's clever thinking!
socratic_feedback.generic_incorrect: Imagine the worst luck: 1st pick is red, 2nd is blue. What MUST the 3rd one be?
socratic_feedback.encouragement_after_retry: Think: with only 2 colour choices, how many picks before one repeats?

author: AP
